SUMMARY:
Industrial applications of engineered enzymes By developing a variety of new and enhanced biocatalysts suitable for a wide range of biocatalysis-based processes in industries, recent developments in genetic engineering techniques, in addition to the introduction of numerous highly efficient computational tools, have revolutionized the field of protein and enzyme engineering. Abstract
--------

Protein engineering mechanisms can be an efficient approach to enhance the biochemical properties of various [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Immobilization of [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the introduction of new-to-nature [chemical reactivities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/chemical-reactivity "Learn more about chemical reactivities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are also possible through the same mechanism. Discovering new protocols that enhance the catalytic active protein that possesses novelty in terms of being stable, active, and, [stereoselectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/stereoselectivity "Learn more about stereoselectivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with functions could be identified as essential areas in terms of concurrent [bioorganic chemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/bioorganic-chemistry "Learn more about bioorganic chemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (synergistic relationship between [organic chemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/organic-chemistry "Learn more about organic chemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [biochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biochemistry "Learn more about biochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the context of enzyme engineering). However, with our current level of knowledge about [protein folding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/protein-folding "Learn more about protein folding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and its correlation with protein conformation and activities, it is almost impossible to [design proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-design "Learn more about design proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with specific biological and physical properties. Hence, contemporary protein engineering typically involves reprogramming existing [enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by [mutagenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/mutagenesis "Learn more about mutagenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to generate new phenotypes with desired properties. These processes ensure that limitations of naturally occurring enzymes are not encountered. For example, researchers have engineered [cellulases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellulase "Learn more about cellulases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and hemicellulases to withstand harsh conditions encountered during biomass [pretreatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/pretreatment "Learn more about pretreatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as high temperatures and acidic environments. By enhancing the activity and robustness of these enzymes, biofuel production becomes more economically viable and environmentally sustainable. Recent trends in [enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have enabled the development of tailored [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [pharmaceutical applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/pharmaceutical-application "Learn more about pharmaceutical applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). For instance, researchers have engineered enzymes such as [cytochrome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cytochrome "Learn more about cytochrome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") P450s and [amine oxidases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/amine-oxidase-flavin-containing "Learn more about amine oxidases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to catalyze challenging reactions involved in drug synthesis. In addition to conventional methods, there has been an increasing application of machine learning techniques to identify patterns in data. These patterns are then used to predict protein structures, enhance enzyme solubility, stability, and function, forecast [substrate specificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/substrate-specificity "Learn more about substrate specificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and assist in rational protein design. In this review, we discussed recent trends in [enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to optimize the biochemical properties of various [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Using examples relevant to biotechnology in engineering enzymes, we try to expatiate the significance of enzyme engineering with how these methods could be applied to optimize the biochemical properties of a naturally occurring enzyme.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about Enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are proteinous biocatalyzers that regulate intricate metabolic pathways and other cellular functions in addition to determining the rate of biological reactions [[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib2), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib3), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib4)]. Enzymes as [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are known to exhibit high efficiency and versatility. Reactions proceed far more quickly when enzymes are present than when they are not [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib5)]. These biological molecules can identify the chemicals that require transformation with extreme specificity. Over the course of development, enzymes have evolved on their own, giving rise to innovative [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with superior qualities and a wide range of biological applications [[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib8)]. All forms of life are now capable of undergoing a vast range of alterations because of their regulated evolution over time. Throughout the course of evolutionary history, all forms of life have undergone continuous adaptation and modification, leading to the development of diverse biological traits and capabilities. This evolutionary process, driven by [natural selection](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/natural-selection "Learn more about natural selection from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") variation, has enabled organisms to acquire a wide array of characteristics that enhance their survival and [reproductive success](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/reproductive-success "Learn more about reproductive success from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in changing environments. As a result of this regulated evolution over time, organisms possess inherent flexibility and adaptability, allowing them to undergo a vast range of alterations in response to environmental pressures, [genetic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-mutation "Learn more about genetic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and other factors. This capacity for adaptation is a fundamental feature of life on Earth, shaping the diversity and resilience of living organisms across different ecosystems and evolutionary time scales. This emphasizes the dynamic nature of evolution and the role it plays in shaping the biological diversity and adaptability observed in all forms of life. As green [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), they are widely used in a wide range of industries, including baking, beverages, food, textiles, leather, feed, pharmaceuticals, and detergents [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib10)]. This is because they can reduce the use of [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/natural-resource "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), minimize [adverse effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/adverse-event "Learn more about adverse effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on the environment, increase efficiency, be economically viable, and produce high-quality products [[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib8),[[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib13)]. Furthermore, [microbial enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microbial-enzyme "Learn more about microbial enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have the ability to either convert or degrade harmful chemical substances found in household and industrial waste, including [nitriles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nitrile "Learn more about nitriles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), amines, phenolics, and [xenobiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/xenobiotic-agent "Learn more about xenobiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib2),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib7),[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib14)]. Microbial and plant-based enzymes like [hydrolases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/hydrolase "Learn more about hydrolases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and pectinases have been utilized extensively in manufacturing food items like juice, milk and its derivatives, beer, and bread [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib15)]. Highly selective, effective, and stable enzymes have been developed for widespread application in various industries, including detergent, textiles, pharmaceutical synthesis, waste treatment in bioremediation, pulp and paper. Enzymes isolated from natural sources or in their native forms may fall short of what customers in the market need.

The main challenge with enzyme-based catalysis is the lack of effective [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for numerous essential chemical processes with commercial relevance [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib16),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib17)]. Natural biocatalysts are limited in industrial processes by the possibility that it will be extremely inefficient when catalyzing reactions with non-cognate substrates and vulnerable to the severe conditions of chemical reactions in laboratories. Therefore, enhancing the biocatalytic efficacy of native enzymes under chemical reaction settings by higher orders of [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") represents the ultimate challenge in biocatalysis-assisted chemical reactions. This problem is solved via enzymatic engineering [[[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib19)]. [Enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about Enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is now frequently used in some situations to increase the activity of enzymes and satisfy the demands of intricate industrial applications. As a result, great efforts are being made to modify enzymes in order to meet industry demands. Biocatalysts' resistance to high temperatures, severe pH values, and high levels of [organic solvents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/organic-solvents "Learn more about organic solvents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been increased by the application of enzyme engineering [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib16),[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib18),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib20)]. [Enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about Enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques are also frequently used to engineer other features of enzymes, such as specific activity, [substrate specificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/substrate-specificity "Learn more about substrate specificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and binding, and enantio, regio, and [stereo selectivities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/stereoselectivity "Learn more about stereo selectivities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Additionally, enzyme engineering, can be used to simultaneously improve the kinetics and stability of the enzyme (catalytic enzyme characteristics) and the yield of the enzyme and ease of [downstream processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/downstream-processing "Learn more about downstream processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (enzyme productivity). Furthermore, the most recent advancement in enzyme technology is enzyme engineering, which uses different genetic engineering techniques to target several biological molecules' properties at once [[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib21), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib22), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib23)]. Unfortunately, determining which mutations are essential when developing a novel enzyme is difficult [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib24)], which extends the time needed to engineer proteins [[[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib25), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28)]. By developing a variety of new and enhanced biocatalysts suitable for a wide range of biocatalysis-based processes in industries, recent developments in genetic engineering techniques, in addition to the introduction of numerous highly efficient computational tools, have revolutionized the field of protein and enzyme engineering. Most biocatalysis-based sectors, including the [pharmaceutical industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/pharmaceutical-industry "Learn more about pharmaceutical industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), will likely rely on highly efficient and specifically designed enzymes in the near future to produce [bulk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/volume "Learn more about bulk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and fine chemicals [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib22),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib29)]. [Lactobacillus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lactobacillus "Learn more about Lactobacillus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[kefir](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kefir "Learn more about kefir from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[ketoreductases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ketoreductase "Learn more about ketoreductases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (KRED) are employed to catalyze the conversion of a [ketone](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ketone "Learn more about ketone from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") intermediate into a chiral alcohol by reduction. Ketoreductase-mediated processes are employed in the production of intermediates for diverse pharmaceuticals, including montelukast, [atorvastatin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/atorvastatin "Learn more about atorvastatin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [duloxetine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/duloxetine "Learn more about duloxetine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [ezetimibe](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ezetimibe "Learn more about ezetimibe from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [crizotinib](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/crizotinib "Learn more about crizotinib from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [phenylephrine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/phenylephrine "Learn more about phenylephrine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib30),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib31)]. The process involved utilizing a specifically engineered enzyme called R-selective [transaminase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transaminase "Learn more about transaminase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which was produced through directed evolution. This enzyme was employed to carry out the asymmetric [amination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amination "Learn more about amination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of prositagliplitin ketone, resulting in the production of [sitagliptin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/sitagliptin "Learn more about sitagliptin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with an [enantiopurity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/enantiopurity "Learn more about enantiopurity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of 99.95% [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28)]. Abstract
--------

Click to copy section link Section link copied!

![Image 9: Abstract Image](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/acs.jafc.5c03673/asset/images/medium/jf5c03673_0004.gif)

Enzyme preparations are highly valued in various industries due to their specificity and eco-friendliness. However, natural enzymes often face challenges in extreme pH conditions due to their narrow optimal pH ranges. This limitation highlights the need for enzyme modification that can expand their pH adaptability, enhance stability and activity across varying pH levels, and ultimately increase their industrial utility. To address these challenges, recent advancements in protein engineering have equipped scientists with powerful tools to tailor enzymatic properties. Techniques such as rational design, directed evolution, and computational biology now allow for the precise manipulation of enzyme structures and their functional dynamics. These strategies aim to bridge the inherent limitations of natural enzymes with the stringent requirements of industrial processes, especially in environments in which pH levels can fluctuate significantly. This article focuses on the field of protein engineering and provides a comprehensive review of the latest advancements in modification strategies for acid-tolerant and alkali-tolerant enzymes. It covers a variety of aspects including sequence-guided bioinformatics analysis, structural modifications, regulation of the endogenous force microenvironment, and surface engineering. This study aims to offer diverse perspectives and technical support to help enzymes adapt to the complex and ever-changing landscape of industrial applications.

# Breaking Natureâs Limits: Enzyme Design for a Sustainable Future Powered by Intelligent Architectureâ¢

# Breaking Natureâs Limits: Enzyme Design for a Sustainable Future Powered by Intelligent Architectureâ¢

The growing demand for new enzymes is catalyzing the evolution of industrial biotechnology, biopharmaceuticals, bioremediation, and R&D while simultaneously driving the transformation to sustainable manufacturing processes.

As the worldâs population continues to grow, there is an urgent need for enzyme-based solutions that can redefine manufacturing and provide healthy, nutritious food more efficiently with lower environmental impact. These demands have spurred researchers and companies to push the boundaries beyond what was once thought possible with enzyme engineering.

â

### Reimagining Enzyme Design

Until recently, the strategy for developing industrial enzymes relied on identifying those found in nature and enhancing them via incremental improvements through protein engineering. This approach, however, results in âFrankensteinâ enzymes that are not always suited for the demands of industrial processes.

To transform industries and meet global sustainability goals, we must transcend the limits of working with natural enzymes alone and redesign the fundamental structures and functions of enzymes, rewriting natureâs catalytic tools for yet-to-be-imagined applications.

Biomatter has reimagined enzyme design in response to these complexities, taking a radically different approach: starting with the ideal end state in mind. That means defining clear objectives, requirements, constraints, and acceptance criteria. It means designing enzymes that exhibit precise function and performance characteristicsâthe specific chemistries and functions tailored to the specific applications. This shift from identifying and modifying to a *de novo*, ground-up design approach marks a pivotal change in biotechnological innovation.

â

### Pioneering Generative AI Enzyme Design

Starting in 2019, [Biomatter](https://biomatter.ai/) pioneered the use of generative AI to expand the protein sequence space by developing [ProteinGAN](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/789719v2), a specialized variant of the generative adversarial network that can learn natural protein sequence diversity and enable the generation of functional protein sequences.

Since then, Biomatter has perfected proprietary generative AI tools and physics engines to design enzymes from the ground up, moving beyond the constraints of nature's blueprints.

An example of Biomatterâs enzyme engineering and precision fermentation prowess is its recent collaboration with Kirin Holdings to increase the production of [Lacto-N-fucopentaose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1096717623001829?via%3Dihub) (LNFP I), the second most abundant fucosylated human milk oligosaccharide (HMO) in breast milk after 2'-Fucosyllactose (2'-FL). Until now, producing LNFP I at a high titer without producing 2'-FL has been a challenge.

LNFP I is produced through an enzymatic reaction catalyzed by the enzyme Î±1,2-fucosyltransferase (Î±1,2-FucT).

Biomatter redesigned the substrate binding pocket of Î±1,2-fucosyltransferase (FsFucT) and the hydrophobic residues on the enzyme's surface. The resulting enzyme selectively produced LNFP I compared to 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL) and was optimized for batch-fed fermentation.

As a result of this work, the strain expressing the newly redesigned FsFucT enzyme showed increased accumulation of LNFP I and only produced a trace amount of 2'-FL. The ability to decrease the amount of by-products is essential when manufacturing on an industrial scale.

â

### Addressing Perceived Risks

While embracing AI for enzyme design offers unprecedented potential, it also introduces certain perceived risks and limitations that merit consideration. These include:

It is important to recognize these considerations not as insurmountable obstacles but as integral aspects of the current innovation landscape in synthetic biology. These challenges can be overcome by supporting AI with physics and empirical scientific inquiry to refine and validate designs.

Biomatter uses fully experimentally validated AI and physics-based software tools to address the above challenges. It showcases that designed enzymes can be developed, tested, and produced quickly. Our methodology erases the boundaries between the conceivable and the achievable, the possible and the impossible.

Biomatter's enzyme design capabilities enable near-perfect control over enzyme design, making previously impossible technical jumps possible while opening the door to unique enzyme-based products and technologies. The company develops tailor-made enzymes with unique functions that are fully optimized for industrial applications by increasing activity, specificity, stability, and expression levels by functionalizing nonfunctional scaffolds and discovering new proteins on accelerated timelines.

We are on the cusp of revolutionizing protein engineering, entering an era where researchers are not constrained by nature's initial designs but can create bespoke, fit-for-purpose enzymes.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Bioinformatics advancements enhance enzyme engineering by analyzing sequences and structures, enabling precise modifications and predictions of enzyme behavior in various industrial applications.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of AI and Machine Learning
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI and machine learning are transforming enzyme design by predicting enzyme functionalities, optimizing processes, and accelerating the identification of effective biocatalysts for industrial use.
Predictive models leverage computational tools to forecast enzyme properties such as stability, activity, and substrate specificity, thereby streamlining the enzyme design process.
Innovative techniques in enzyme engineering, including directed evolution and rational design, facilitate faster development cycles for optimized industrial enzymes tailored to specific applications.

The integration of modern enzyme engineering techniques promises to revolutionize sectors like pharmaceuticals, biofuels, and bioremediation, leading to more efficient and sustainable industrial processes.
